column1: In India, the growth of modern nationalism is intimately connected to which movement?
column2: The anti-colonial movement.

column1: Why did many different social groups in India find a shared bond under colonialism?
column2: They shared a sense of being oppressed.

column1: Term: Satyagraha
column2: A novel method of mass agitation based on the power of truth and non-violence.

column1: When did Mahatma Gandhi return to India from South Africa?
column2: January 1915.

column1: How did the First World War affect the economic situation in India?
column2: It led to huge increases in defense expenditure, taxes, and prices.

column1: Cloze: The process by which the colonial state forced people to join the army is called _____.
column2: forced recruitment

column1: According to the census of 1921, how many people perished due to famines and the influenza epidemic?
column2: 12 to 13 million.

column1: Concept: Non-violence (Dharma)
column2: Definition: The supreme moral duty that Mahatma Gandhi believed could unite all Indians.

column1: Where did Mahatma Gandhi organize his first satyagraha movement in 1917 to support peasants?
column2: Champaran, Bihar.

column1: What was the specific cause of the 1917 satyagraha in the Kheda district of Gujarat?
column2: Peasants were unable to pay revenue due to crop failure and a plague epidemic.

column1: Which group did Mahatma Gandhi support in the 1918 Ahmedabad satyagraha?
column2: Cotton mill workers.

column1: What powers did the Rowlatt Act (1919) give the British government?
column2: The power to repress political activities and detain prisoners without trial for two years.

column1: On what date did the Rowlatt satyagraha begin with a nationwide hartal?
column2: 6 April 1919.

column1: Who was the British officer in command during the Jallianwalla Bagh incident?
column2: General Dyer.

column1: What was General Dyer's stated objective for the Jallianwalla Bagh massacre?
column2: To 'produce a moral effect' and create a feeling of terror and awe in satyagrahis.

column1: Which movement was launched to bring Hindus and Muslims closer together in a unified national struggle?
column2: The Khilafat movement.

column1: Term: Khalifa
column2: The spiritual head of the Islamic world (the Ottoman emperor).

column1: Who were the two main Muslim leaders of the Khilafat Committee formed in March 1919?
column2: Muhammad Ali and Shaukat Ali.

column1: In which book did Mahatma Gandhi declare that British rule survived only because of Indian cooperation?
column2: Hind Swaraj (1909).

column1: At which Congress session was the Non-Cooperation programme finally adopted in December 1920?
column2: The Nagpur session.

column1: Cloze: A form of demonstration by which people block the entrance to a shop or factory is called _____.
column2: picketing

column1: What dramatic economic effect did the Non-Cooperation Movement have on foreign cloth imports by 1922?
column2: The value of imports dropped from Rs 102 crore to Rs 57 crore.

column1: Why did the Non-Cooperation Movement in cities gradually slow down regarding clothing?
column2: Poor people could not afford the more expensive khadi cloth.

column1: Who led the peasant movement in Awadh against talukdars and landlords?
column2: Baba Ramchandra.

column1: Cloze: Labour that villagers were forced to contribute without any payment is known as _____.
column2: begar

column1: What were the primary demands of the Awadh peasant movement?
column2: Reduction of revenue, abolition of begar, and social boycott of oppressive landlords.

column1: Which organization was set up in October 1920 to address peasant grievances in Awadh?
column2: Oudh Kisan Sabha.

column1: Who led the militant guerrilla movement in the Gudem Hills of Andhra Pradesh?
column2: Alluri Sitaram Raju.

column1: What was Alluri Sitaram Raju's unconventional view on liberating India?
column2: He believed India could only be liberated by the use of force, not non-violence.

column1: Which 1859 Act prevented plantation workers in Assam from leaving tea gardens without permission?
column2: The Inland Emigration Act.

column1: What 1922 incident led Mahatma Gandhi to call a halt to the Non-Cooperation Movement?
column2: The violent clash at Chauri Chaura in Gorakhpur.

column1: Which two leaders formed the Swaraj Party within the Congress to argue for council politics?
column2: C. R. Das and Motilal Nehru.

column1: Why was the Statutory Commission under Sir John Simon opposed by all Indian parties?
column2: It did not have a single Indian member.

column1: In December 1929, the Lahore Congress formalized the demand for what?
column2: Purna Swaraj (Full Independence).

column1: What date was chosen to be celebrated as 'Independence Day' in 1930 to pledge for complete independence?
column2: 26 January.

column1: Why did Mahatma Gandhi choose salt as a symbol for the Civil Disobedience Movement?
column2: It was an essential item consumed by both the rich and the poor.

column1: The Salt March covered 240 miles from Sabarmati to which coastal town?
column2: Dandi.

column1: How many trusted volunteers accompanied Mahatma Gandhi on the Salt March?
What act marked the beginning of the Civil Disobedience Movement on 6 April 1930?
column2: Gandhi ceremonially manufactured salt by boiling sea water at Dandi.

column1: How did the Civil Disobedience Movement differ from the Non-Cooperation Movement in its instructions to people?
column2: People were asked not only to refuse cooperation but also to break colonial laws.

column1: Who was the 'Frontier Gandhi' arrested in Peshawar in April 1930?
column2: Abdul Ghaffar Khan.

column1: Name the 1931 agreement where Gandhi consented to a Round Table Conference and the government released political prisoners.
column2: The Gandhi-Irwin Pact.

column1: Why did rich peasant communities like the Patidars and Jats participate in the Civil Disobedience Movement?
column2: They were hard hit by the trade depression and sought a reduction in government revenue.

column1: Which organization was formed in 1927 to organize business interests in India?
column2: The Federation of the Indian Chamber of Commerce and Industries (FICCI).

column1: Why did industrial workers generally stay away from the Civil Disobedience Movement?
column2: The Congress was seen as being too close to the industrialists.

column1: What role did women play in the Civil Disobedience Movement?
column2: They participated in protest marches, manufactured salt, and picketed shops.

column1: Term: Harijan
column2: The name Mahatma Gandhi gave to 'untouchables', meaning 'children of God'.

column1: Which leader organized Dalits into the Depressed Classes Association in 1930?
column2: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar.

column1: What was the outcome of the 1932 Poona Pact?
column2: Reserved seats were given to Depressed Classes in legislative councils, to be voted by the general electorate.

column1: Why were some Muslim political organizations lukewarm toward the Civil Disobedience Movement?
column2: They feared their minority culture would be submerged under a Hindu majority.

column1: Who first created the visual image of Bharat Mata in the twentieth century?
column2: Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay.

column1: Which novel by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay included the hymn 'Vande Mataram'?
column2: Anandmath.

column1: How did Abanindranath Tagore's 1905 painting portray Bharat Mata?
column2: As an ascetic figure: calm, composed, divine, and spiritual.

column1: What was the significance of Gandhi's 1921 Swaraj flag design?
column2: It was a tricolour with a spinning wheel representing self-help.

column1: Term: HSRA
column2: Hindustan Socialist Republican Army, founded in 1928 to struggle against British power through revolution.

column1: Which 1928 satyagraha in Gujarat, led by Vallabhbhai Patel, was a success against land revenue enhancement?
column2: The Bardoli Satyagraha.

column1: Which prominent leader died after being assaulted by British police during a protest against the Simon Commission?
column2: Lala Lajpat Rai.
